coredata.dc:title: Incorporating of gold nanoclusters into metal-organic frameworks for highly sensitive detection of 3-nitrotyrosine as an oxidative stress biomarker
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Jalili, Roghayeh
Dastborhan, Mahsa
Chenaghlou, Salimeh
Khataee, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: 3-nitrotyrosine (3NT) level in biological samples, considered as an appropriate biomarker for nitrosative stress, is associated with many inflammatory and chronic diseases. In this research, a novel highly fluorescent hybrid material (AuNCs@ZIF-8) has been synthesized by encapsulating gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) into the zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8). Due to the confinement-assisted emission enhancement, the quantum yield of AuNCs@ZIF-8 attained to 36.6 %, which was nearly 15 times that of the sole AuNCs. The obtained AuNCs@ZIF-8 composites were used to develop an ultrasensitive and highly selective sensor for 3NT. In comparison with sensing by pure AuNCs without the amplifying effect of ZIF-8, the developed AuNCs@ZIF-8 based sensor indicated almost two orders of magnitude lower limit of detection (LOD), and 3NT could be detected in the range of 5−200 nM with a detection limit of 1.8 nM (S/N = 3). The developed sensor was applied to quantify 3NT in biological samples and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3-Nitrotyrosine
Gold nanocluster
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Nanosensor
Confinement effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Practical application of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for determination of 3NT in biological samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HR-TEM image of AuNCs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs (blue curve), AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite (red curve), and pure ZIF-8 (yellow curve) (under excitation at 400 nm(. The inset image shows the digital photographs of (A) AuNCs, (B) AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite and (C) pure ZIF-8 under UV light of 365 nm, (C) TEM image of AuNCs@ZIF-8 (inset: the corresponding close-up TEM image of a single particle) and (D) SEM image of AuNCs@ZIF-8.
XRD patterns of ZIF-8 and AuNCs@ZIF-8.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@ZIF-8 system upon the exposure of various concentrations of 3NT, and (b) calibration regression curve (Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements). The experimental conditions were pH = 5.0 under room temperature and 8 min incubation time.
Signal recovery after 10 times measurement of 100 nM of 3NT (regenerated by using water at pH = 12). The experimental conditions were pH = 5.0 under room temperature and 8 min incubation time.
Schematic representation of the fabrication route for AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite as a probe for 3NT detection.
Tolerance limits of diverse ions and biological substances in the determination of 100 nM of 3NT under optimum conditions.

row.entry: Na+, K+, NH4+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, F−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2-, PO4 3-
L-Cysteine, Alanine, Glycine, Tryptophan, Histidine, Creatinine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of 3NT in spiked human serum and urine samples.
Comparative study between the proposed method and other published methods for the 3NT quantification.

head.ce:title: Incorporating of gold nanoclusters into metal-organic frameworks for highly sensitive detection of 3-nitrotyrosine as an oxidative stress biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Gebze Technical University, 41400, Gebze, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, Gebze Technical University, 41400 Gebze, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University, Da Nang, 550000, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University, Da Nang 550000, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication route for AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite as a probe for 3NT detection.

3-nitrotyrosine (3NT) level in biological samples, considered as an appropriate biomarker for nitrosative stress, is associated with many inflammatory and chronic diseases. In this research, a novel highly fluorescent hybrid material (AuNCs@ZIF-8) has been synthesized by encapsulating gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) into the zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8). Due to the confinement-assisted emission enhancement, the quantum yield of AuNCs@ZIF-8 attained to 36.6 %, which was nearly 15 times that of the sole AuNCs. The obtained AuNCs@ZIF-8 composites were used to develop an ultrasensitive and highly selective sensor for 3NT. In comparison with sensing by pure AuNCs without the amplifying effect of ZIF-8, the developed AuNCs@ZIF-8 based sensor indicated almost two orders of magnitude lower limit of detection (LOD), and 3NT could be detected in the range of 5−200 nM with a detection limit of 1.8 nM (S/N = 3). The developed sensor was applied to quantify 3NT in biological samples and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive nitrogen species are produced by inflammatory cells and cause the nitration of tyrosine to form 3-nitrotyrosine (3NT) [1,2]. In biological samples, an increase in 3NT level is critical signaling marker that displays the progression of inflammatory disorders thus heightened level of 3NT associated with various diseases such as diabetes, Alzheimer, atherosclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, psychiatric disorders, cardiovascular diseases, diseases associated with immunological reactions and neurological diseases [3–5]. As a result, 3NT serves as a preferred biomarker for nitrosative stress and developing sensitive, selective, and accurate methods for monitoring it in biological samples is of great importance. Three principle methodologies including mass spectrometry (MS) approaches, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7] have been carried out for the detection and quantification of 3NT in samples. Up to now, several reviews dealing with approaches for determination of 3NT in different matrices have appeared [8–10].

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) composed of a few tens of atoms haveattained much attention as fluorescent nanomaterials for biomedicine, bioimaging and sensing. Size-dependent optical properties of fluorescent AuNCs are similar to those of quantum dots and show advantages such as easy conjugation to biomolecules, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. However, AuNCs fluorescent quantum yield (QY) is much lower than those of the traditional chromophores (e.g. quantum dots and organic dyes) [11–13]. Recently, efforts have been made to enhance the fluorescence intensity and stability of AuNCs, using mechanisms like aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [12,14–16] and restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) [17–19]. However, new biocompatible, simple and rapid procedures are still in high demand to improve the fluorescence intensity and stability of AuNCs.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are hybrid materials which are comprised of metallic ions and organic ligands [20]. The unique properties of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) such as uniform and tunable pores size, ultrahigh porosity, and good chemical and thermal stabilities make them excellent choices for various applications. Embedding guest materials into MOF structure as host materials is an innovative method to create functionalized MOF-based composites with enhanced performance characteristics of the guest materials. Interesting advanced materials and novel applications have been achieved by encapsulating dyes [21], polymers [22], biomolecules [23] and particularly nanomaterials [24] within MOFs. But encapsulation of fluorescent metal nanoclusters into MOFs to enhance the fluorescence intensity and stability of nanoclusters was rarely reported. In pioneering work, Cao’s group confined AuNCs into zeolitic-imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) to improve the QY of AuNCs and utilized the composite in real-time monitoring of drug release [25]. In another work, the crystalline ion triggered growth method was used for incorporation of AuNCs into ZIF-8. It was shown that the QY and fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs were markedly increased after encapsulating in ZIF-8. They applied the obtained composite for the detection of H2S in liquid and gas phases [26].

Compared to formerly reported MOF hosts, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is an ideal selection. ZIF-8 has a symmetrical porous structure analogous to zeolite and shows high surface area and thermal and chemical stability. ZIF-8 is prepared via the coordination of zinc ions with 2-methyl imidazole at room temperature and considered as superb support for the uniform encapsulation of fluorescent nanoparticles [24].

Herein, the AuNCs were introduced into ZIF-8 cavities by one-pot procedure that mixes the AuNCs and ZIF-8. Compared with the sole AuNCs, resultant composite (AuNCs@ ZIF-8) showed about 15-fold enhancement in the fluorescent QY. 3NT selectively quenched the fluorescence of AuNCs due to specific electron-withdrawing interactions with the amino groups of the BSA on AuNCs. As shown in Scheme 1 , this phenomenon was applied for selective and sensitive detection of 3NT. The novel MOF based sensor exhibited higher sensitivity and selectivity towards 3NT than AuNCs alone. The sensor was used for sensitive and selective detection of 3NT in biological samples in concentrations low enough to diagnose many diseases.

Fluorescence spectra and intensities were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1 cm × 1 cm). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by the Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by a Zeiss (Leo 906, and Germany). The High -resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were collected at 200 kV by JEM-2200FS (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed with Mira3 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Czech Republic). Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer from KBr pellets of the sample (Bruker Co., Germany). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured via nitrogen adsorption/desorption at 77 K by a Quantachrome C Instrument (Chem BET-3000, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were scanned by a Siemens D5000 X-ray diffractometer (Germany).

All reagents were of analytical grade and used as supplied without purification. 3-nitrotyrosine (3NT) was obtained from Alfa-Aesar (Massachusetts, U.S.). Tetrachloroauric (III) acid (HAuCl4.3H2O, 99.99 %), bovine serum albumin (BSA, > 95 %) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

The AuNCs were synthesized via a green method by using BSA as a reducing and protecting agent according to previously reported methods [27]. Briefly, 2.5 mL of 10 mM aqueous solution of HAuCl4 was added to the BSA solution (2.5 mL, 50 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring. After adjusting the pH at 11 by the addition of NaOH solution (1 M) to the above mixture, the obtained yellow solution was kept at 37 °C for 8 h. The color of the solution turned to deep brown. The pH of the final suspension was adjusted at 7.0. The prepared AuNCs suspension was kept at 4 °C for future use.

The AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was prepared according to the literature [28]. Briefly, 10 mL of Zn (NO3)2.6H2O (0.987 mmol) was added to 10 mL of 2-methylimidazole (7.904 mmol) aqueous solutions under vigorous magnetic stirring. The resultant suspension was centrifuged, and ZIF-8 with nanoparticles were collected and washed thoroughly three times with water. The resultant participate was dispersed in 50 mL deionized water. Then, 10 mL of AuNCs solution was added to the above ZIF-8 suspension. After 1 h incubation, the AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite nanoparticles were separated using a centrifuge and washed three times with water and redispersed in 100 mL water.

In a 4.0-mL standard cuvette, 150 μL of stock solution of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite, 50 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 8, 0.5 M) and appropriate amounts of 3NT solution were mixed and the final volume was reached to 4 mL with water and incubated for 8 min. the fluorescence intensities were measured at 625 nm with the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. To evaluate the ZIF-8 effect on sensitivity and selectivity enhancement, the control experiments were done by the same procedure using sole AuNCs suspension instead of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite.

Human urine and serum samples were obtained from apparently healthy subjects and were stored at −20 °C until analysis. Urine and serum samples spiked with three different concentration levels of 3NT (30, 50, 100 nM). To precipitate proteins, 0.5 mL acetonitrile was added to 1 mL of each serum or urine sample and vortexed for 20 s. Then, samples were centrifuged at 6000 (rpm) for 15 min. Afterward, the supernatant was extracted and filtered through a filter membrane device (0.5 μm). The clear supernatant was diluted to 5.0 mL and the 3NT concentration was determined by the general procedure.

The AuNCs were synthesized via a green method using BSA as a reducing and protecting agent and characterized by HR-TEM imaging, fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy analyses. HR-TEM images (Fig. 1 a) showed a uniform size distribution of the synthesized AuNCs with an average size of about 2.0 nm. As shown in Fig. 1b (blue curve), the aqueous solution of the AuNCs exhibited a weak fluorescence with a maximum at 625 nm when excited at 400 nm. The AuNCs showed a pale red emission when exposed to UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1b), and the QYs is calculated to be 1.1 % using fluorescein (basic ethanol solution, QY = 97 %) as a reference. As shown in Fig. S1 (Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM), the UV–vis absorption spectra of the AuNCs displayed an absorption peak at 500 nm suggesting that the particle size was smaller than 2.5 nm [17].

AuNCs@ZIF-8 probe was synthesized via facile one-pot route by confinement of AuNCs into ZIF-8 MOF. The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was about 15 times higher than the individual AuNCs (Fig. 1b, red curve, with the same concentration of fluorescence species). This effect is attributed to the confinement-assisted emission enhancement like the aggregation-induced emission phenomenon in metal nanoclusters. The AuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibited a strong red emission when exposed to UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1b) and The QY of AuNCs@ZIF-8 was calculated to be 17.14 %, which is greater than the QY of AuNCs (QY = 1.1 %). The QY enhancement in the AuNCs@ZIF-8 is closely correlated with the MOF (ZIF-8) scaffold, mainly because of the following reasons. First, due to confinement effect of ZIF-8, AuNCs were tightly enclosed by ZIF-8, which greatly restrain the vibration and rotation of BSA ligands on the surface of AuNCs thus the majority of excited electrons return to the ground state via the radiative decay rather than nonradiative decay, leading to fluorescence enhancement. Second, the ZIF-8 can markedly reduce the access of quenching molecules to the AuNCs which, then avoid the undesirable solvent relaxation on the fluorescence of AuNCs (Deng et al., 2019). Pure ZIF-8 displayed no prominent fluorescence peak (Fig. 1b, yellow curve) and also showed no fluorescence upon exposure to UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1b) suggesting that fluorescence of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 arises from the AuNCs rather than ZIF-8.

As displayed in TEM images (Fig. 1c), the as-synthesized AuNCs@ZIF-8 particles have a rhombic dodecahedron shape with an average size of about 60 nm. Furthermore, SEM images revealed that the AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite particles have the scales in the range of 40 and 80 nm and show good monodispersity (Fig. 1d).

The crystal structure of synthesized AuNCs@ZIF-8 and pure ZIF-8 were also investigated by powder XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 , both the ZIF-8 and AuNCs@ZIF-8 show the reflection at 2θ = 7.3° related to a 110 peak which agrees well with the fact that the nanoparticles of both ZIF-8 and AuNCs@ZIF-8 are fabricated of 12 [110] facets and have rhombic dodecahedron-shapedcrystals. The XRD pattern of resulting AuNCs@ZIF-8 agreed well with those of the pure ZIF-8 nanoparticles and no additional peaks of AuNCs were observed, indicating that due to the minute concentrations and small sizes of AuNCs hosted by the ZIF-8 matrix, the pure crystalline phase of ZIF-8 was not damaged during the post-synthetic loading modification.

The porosity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was measured by BET gas-sorption device. As shown in Fig. S2, the BET isotherm of AuNCs@ZIF-8 demonstrated a typical type-I isotherm which suggests the dominating microspore characteristics and the BET surface of AuNCs@ZIF-8 was calculated to be 1047 m2 g−1. This BET value agrees well with formerly reported surface area values for ZIF-8 [24].

To obtain better performance of the fluorescence quenching for the detection of 3NT, some experimental conditions were optimized using a quenching amount defined as F0/F, Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of certain amount of 3NT, respectively.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence response of AuNCs@ZIF-8 toward 3NT was investigated in PBS buffers with different pH values ranged from 4.0–9.0. The maximum F0/F value was observed at pH 5.0 (Fig. S3). The more significant F0/F value, the more apparent was the change, and thus, the more sensitive the sensor. Therefore, we chose pH 5 (phosphate buffer solution, 0.1 M) as suitable solvent media for the detection of 3NT.

To get the shortest time of analysis the effect of reaction time on the quenching amount of the probe was also considered. A certain amount of 3NT (100 nM) was mixed with AuNCs@ZIF-8 (15 mg L−1), then the fluorescence intensities were determined at different time intervals. It was found that the quenching amount (F0/F) increased rapidly after the addition of 3NT at initial 8 min, then it remained constant in the following 20 min (Fig. S4). Thus, 8.0 min was selected as the optimal interaction time.

The effect of ZIF-8/AuNCs ratio on fluorescence enhancement was investigated by fixing the amount of AuNCs (10 mL) and increasing the amount of ZIF-8. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the more considerable fluorescence enhancement was obtained with 50 mL of ZIF-8. The low ZIF-8/AuNCs ratios would result in incomplete encapsulation of AuNCs.

The fluorescence intensities of AuNCs@ZIF-8 gradually decreased with increasing of 3NT concentrations successively (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3b there was excellent linearity (with a correlation coefficient of 0.997) for the quenching amount, defined as F0/F, against the concentration of 3NT in the concentration range from 5 to 200 nM. The detection limit (LOD) of this system was calculated to be 1.8 nM (S/N = 3) which meets with the requirement of trace analysis of 3NT at biological levels [5,29]. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 3 identical quantifications of 100 nM of 3NT was found to be 1.8 %, which indicates the good repeatability of the Au-NCs@ZIF-8 sensor. To validate the advantages of AuNCs encapsulating in the MOF structure, sole AuNCs were used for the analysis of 3NT as control. In optimum condition, the linear range of 400–3000 nM and the LOD of 123 nM were obtained for those sole AuNCs. As can be seen, the LOD of AuNCs@ZIF-8 based method was lower by two orders of magnitude than sole AuNCs. Therefore, the high fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and the role of the ZIF-8 MOFs in accumulating of targets were expected to provide excellent sensitivity to target molecules. In order to assay the enrichment effect of the AuNCs@ZIF-8 to 3NT, the AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite (1 mL) was mixed with 3 mL solution of 3NT (0.1 M) and stirred for 5 min. After centrifugation, the concentration of 3NT in the supernatant was checked by UV–vis instrument. The concentration reduced to 0.003 M in supernatant which suggests that AuNCs@ZIF-8 can enrich) adsorb (3NT significantly that originated from the well-defined porosity of MOF.

Under the influence of the nitro group (strong electron acceptor) in the molecular structure of 3NT, it has a strong electron acceptor property. 3NT can interact with primary amines (electron donors) via the strong p-donor (amine)-p-acceptor interaction. On the other hand, BSA, as a large protein has a high essential amino acid profile. Therefore, the binding of electron-deficient 3NT to the amino groups in the BSA facilitates the formation of Meisenheimer complexes between them. This proximity results in an efficient electron transfer process from the amino group to the aromatic ring of 3NT, which in turn causes strong fluorescence suppression [30]. It is worth to mention that the energy transfer is not a probable mechanism for fluorescence decrease because there is no overlap between the absorption spectrum of the 3NT and the fluorescence spectrum of the AuNCs (625 nm) (Fig. S6).

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system for 3NT over some potentially interfering biomolecules and ions, the fluorescence response of the nanohybrid probe for 100 nM 3NT in presence of various interfering substances including Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, F−, Cl-, NO3-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, sucrose, glucose, maltose, lactose, histidine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glutathione, l-cysteine, glycine, tryptophan and creatinine were carefully examined. The tolerance limits were considered as the concentration of the interfering substances causing a relative error of less than 5 %. The results (Table 1 ) clearly demonstrated an excellent selectivity of the sensing system for 3NT. The remarkable selectivity of the method is the result of using the ZIF-8 structure with superior size-selectivity that significantly reduces the interference from large molecules. These findings demonstrate that this highly selective and sensitive nanohybrid probe can be used for detection of 3NT in biological fluids at trace concentrations.

Reusability is one of the critical factors for the practical application of sensors. For evaluation of the reusability of the AuNCs@ZIF-8, the same AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was used to detect 3NT (100 nM) through the general procedure. After each measurement, the AuNCs@ZIF-8 were washed with water (pH = 12) to remove the 3NT molecules. During the repetition of the removal−rebinding cycles five times, the overall fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 decreased only by 6 % compared to that of as-prepared AuNCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 4 ) which confirms the high reproducibility and physical and chemical resistance of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of AuNCs@ZIF-8 for determination of 3NT in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the developed method for quantification of 3NT in biological samples, the developed probe was applied to the quantification of 3NT in human urine and serum samples. The experimental results are shown in Table 2 . For each sample, three parallel experiments were performed. The obtained recoveries were in the range of 97.6–100.9 % with a relative standard deviation of less than 2.1 %, suggesting the high accuracy and potential applicability of the present sensor for 3NT quantitative determination in real samples.

The performance of our proposed method was compared with some previously reported techniques for 3NT detection. As listed in Table 3 , not only our proposed method is appealing because of its simplicity, low cost and no need for antibodies, but also the LOD of this method is on the same order as ELISA (the most common and commercialized method for 3NT detection [29,31]. The LOD of the proposed method was lower than those of the chromatographic techniques [6,10]. Meanwhile, seldom does this method need tedious and time-consuming pre-separation procedures, high purity and expensive solvents and sophisticated apparatus. Although the lower detection limit (0.027 μg L−1) for 3NT was achieved by using LC–MS/MS method, LC–MS/MS technique is a costly, sophisticated, elaborated and time-consuming procedure [32]. Compared with other florescent approaches [3], interestingly, this developed method avoided complex synthesis process, just needed to mix the as-prepared AuCs and ZIF-8 within 1 h for forming the highly sensitive and selective sensor.

In summary, we prepared highly luminescent nanocomposites (AuNCs@ZIF-8) by encapsulating AuNCs into ZIF-8 via a facile and one-pot procedure. Compared to AuNCs, the AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite exhibited markedly enhanced quantum yields. The bright red emission of AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was selectively quenched by 3NT probably via photoinduced electron transfer process. Comparison of the suggested sensor with some other reported analytical methods for the 3NT determination indicates that our probe has comparable sensitivity with some ELISA methods and better sensitivity than some reported HPLC methods. Although the lower detection limit for 3NT was achieved by using LC–MS/MS method, LC–MS/MS technique involve expensive instruments and time-consuming procedures. Compared with other florescent approaches, interestingly, this developed method avoided complex synthesis and analysis processes. The senor has a good selectivity and can be used for determination of nM levels of 3NT in urine and serum samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We would like to confirm that there is no known conflict of interest associated with this publication.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112370.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer–green emitting carbon dot composite for selective and sensitive detection of 3-nitrotyrosine as a biomarker
Sens. Actuators B Chem.
